title: Mineralisation of Atmospheric Aerosol Particles and Trace Element Analysis by ICP-OES
author: Dufour, Migon
materials: ICP multi-element standard solution VIII CertiPUR®
mono-elemental solutions for V and Ti

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: All samples (natural aerosol samples + SRM samples) were dissolved by acid treatment by sets of 54 samples, including two blank filters, and two reagent blanks.•10 mg of NIST 1648 and 1648a were weighed on a filter with a Mettler Toledo XS205 precision balance.•Each filter was folded into 4 and introduced into an acid-cleaned Teflon bottle (60 mL).•The acid mixture was added: Aqua regia + HF (1.5 mL HCl 30% + 0.5 mL HNO3 65% + 0.5 mL HF 40%).•The set of bottles was put in ultrasonic bath during 10 min.•Each Teflon bottle was placed in the block digester and overcome with a cap reflux condenser.•Temperature programmes were set to 130 °C. To avoid the projection of sample particles, the temperature was progressively elevated: 2 °C min−1 to attain 130 °C and be maintained at this temperature during two hours.•A residue was obtained and dissolved with 1.5 mL HNO3 (65%), ultrasonically agitated for 15 min, and made up to volume with 10 mL of Milli-Q® water.•The solution was finally stored in an acid-cleaned polypropylene tube (15 mL) and kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C until analysis.

steps.title: Procedure
steps.instruction: Other tested protocols can be found in the Supplementary material section.

steps.title: Validation of the mineralisation protocol
steps.instruction: Digestion methods were validated by analysing SRM 1648 and 1648a samples.

steps.title: Validation of the mineralisation protocol

steps.title: Validation of the analytical method

steps.title: Validation of the analytical method
steps.instruction: The detection limits were <1 μg L−1 for most metals except Al, K and Pb (<2 μg L−1), and As (5.32 μg L−1).

steps.title: Validation of the analytical method
steps.instruction: The precision of the method was evaluated by measuring NIST 1643e (n = 10) and TM-28.4 for Ti. The%RSD were 1.5 (Al), 1.4 (As), 0.6 (Ba), 5.8 (Cd), 2.35 (Co), 3.43 (Cu), 1.3 (Fe), 1.9 (K), 0.1 (Mg), 0.7 (Mn), 1.5 (Na), 0.9 (Ni), 1.41 (Pb), 5.4 (Ti), 1.8 (V), and 1 (Zn).

description: The analysis of atmospheric aerosol samples collected on filters may sometimes be a difficult challenge, owing to the combined effects of chemically resistant matrices potentially present in the atmospheric aerosol (alumino-silicates, oxides, sea-spray, organic compounds [1]), low quantities of collected material (of the order of few milligrammes, typically) and trace/ultra-trace elemental concentrations encountered (of the order of few μg L−1, typically). Attempts to totally digest these matrices with a single protocol may lead to signal losses, therefore yielding low recoveries and hence underestimated measurements. Non-destructive analytical techniques such as X-ray fluorescence, proton-induced X-ray emission (PIXE) are currently being developed which skip the mineralisation phase, but their detection limits are still too high given the elemental concentrations found in the atmospheric aerosol: below 100 ng m−3, the degree of overestimation by Energy Dispersive X-ray Fluorescence (EDXRF) increases when concentrations decrease [2]. In addition, the non-uniformity of elemental distribution on filters may lead to erroneous results [3]. Therefore, mineralisation remains a crucial, and unavoidable phase in the preparation of natural particulate samples for elemental analysis. The present work proposes an alternative, tentatively all-purpose mineralisation protocol (the splitting of samples into sub-samples by cutting filters is banned because of the non-uniform distribution of matter [4]), followed by an analytical method specifically adapted to trace elements in atmospheric particulate samples.
